message:
docs(inventory): mark legacy deferred-surfaces register as historical, align verification docs with shipped baseline

- DEFERRED_SURFACES.md: add historical-status note redirecting readers to
  the current source-backed registers (DEFERRED_SURFACE_REGISTER,
  RUST_EXPORTED_SURFACE_INVENTORY, SUBSYSTEM_EVIDENCE_MATRIX,
  SELECTIVE_SURFACE_PORTING_QUEUE); update net parity impact to reflect
  broader-than-kernel-only shipped surface; demote footer from canonical
  to historical context
- DEFERRED_SURFACE_REGISTER.md: reword crate-level truth table audit
  intro to acknowledge current shipped surface is broader than the earlier
  kernel-only closure implied while noting remaining partial/stub gaps
- SUBSYSTEM_EVIDENCE_MATRIX.md: align pre-implementation contract language
  with current reduced/profile-gated reality instead of absent/stub-only
- .beads/issues.jsonl: add closed beads bd-27hr, bd-geft, bd-yz1y tracking
  the documentation reconciliation work

Co-Authored-By: Claude <noreply@anthropic.com>

 {"id":"bd-296","title":"EPIC: Spec Foundation and Plan-Space Canonicalization","description":"Background:
This project is explicitly spec-first. The C implementation must be derived from extracted semantics, not line-by-line translation from Rust source.

Goal:
Produce a self-contained canonical spec suite so implementation can proceed without consulting legacy Rust internals.

Must cover (non-negotiable):
- COMPLETE lifecycle semantics for region/task/obligation/cancellation
- outcome lattice and budget algebra
- forbidden-behavior catalog (must-fail matrix)
- machine-readable invariant schema
- scenario DSL and fixture contracts
- guarantee-substitution mapping (Rust guarantees -> C mechanisms)
- explicit exclusions/deferred surfaces and open decisions

Why this serves users:
- prevents semantic drift and accidental feature amputation
- enables predictable implementation and review
- allows independent auditors/future maintainers to understand intent without archaeology.

Cross-cutting quality contract:
- Child tasks must define and link unit tests, e2e script coverage (when behavioral), and structured log/manifest artifacts or explicit rationale for non-applicability.
- Completion evidence must remain compatible with bd-66l.9 enforcement and traceability index expectations.","status":"closed","priority":0,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2026-02-27T00:47:41.899905539Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-27T19:36:08.134397338Z","closed_at":"2026-02-27T19:36:08.134377281Z","close_reason":"done","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["phase-0","phase-1","planning","spec"]}
 {"id":"bd-296.1","title":"Create exhaustive docs/EXISTING_ASUPERSYNC_STRUCTURE.md as canonical extracted semantics","description":"Background:
This is the primary extraction artifact and must be complete enough that implementation does not require reading legacy Rust code. It is an aggregation checkpoint over dedicated extraction streams.

Subtasks:
- Integrate outputs from dedicated extraction beads bd-296.15 through bd-296.19 into one canonical implementation-ready document.
- Validate consistency across lifecycle transitions, budget/outcome semantics, channel/timer semantics, and quiescence/cleanup invariants.
- Ensure every extracted claim has provenance references and fixture/parity mappings.

Acceptance Criteria:
- Document is implementation-ready and internally consistent.
- All semantics are concrete (no hand-wavy prose).
- Includes examples for edge/error transitions.
- Completion requires all granular extraction streams (bd-296.15..bd-296.19) to be merged and reconciled.

Detailed logging/evidence:
- Add extraction notes linking each section to fixture IDs to support later audits.","status":"closed","priority":0,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-27T00:48:11.442462982Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-27T04:20:50.900563667Z","closed_at":"2026-02-27T04:20:50.900544962Z","close_reason":"Consolidated exhaustive canonical semantics into docs/EXISTING_ASUPERSYNC_STRUCTURE.md","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["docs","phase-1","spec"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-296.1","depends_on_id":"bd-296","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-03-02T04:37:11Z","created_by":"import","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-296.1","depends_on_id":"bd-296.12","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-02T04:37:11Z","created_by":"import","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-296.1","depends_on_id":"bd-296.15","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-02T04:37:11Z","created_by":"import","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-296.1","depends_on_id":"bd-296.16","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-02T04:37:11Z","created_by":"import","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-296.1","depends_on_id":"bd-296.17","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-02T04:37:11Z","created_by":"import","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-296.1","depends_on_id":"bd-296.18","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-02T04:37:11Z","created_by":"import","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-296.1","depends_on_id":"bd-296.19","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-02T04:37:11Z","created_by":"import","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"bd-296.10","title":"Define Wave A/B/C/D gating protocol and selective-surface expansion criteria","description":"Background:
The wave gating model must enforce both semantic and verification readiness before scope expansion.

Subtasks:
- Define explicit entry/exit criteria for each wave and phase.
- Define criteria for opening Phase 9 selective higher-surface ports.
- Define required spec plus conformance prerequisites for each new subsystem.
- Define mandatory test-evidence package per wave close: unit coverage, invariant coverage, e2e script pass set, and structured log/manifest completeness.

Acceptance Criteria:
- No subsystem enters implementation without documented gate satisfaction.
- Deferred surfaces have explicit unblock conditions.
- Governance process is auditable and repeatable.
- Wave closure fails when required unit/e2e/logging evidence is incomplete.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-27T00:53:09.918448429Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-27T04:28:07.026023748Z","closed_at":"2026-02-27T04:28:07.026005404Z","close_reason":"Added Wave A/B/C/D gating protocol with selective-surface expansion criteria","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["planning","scope","waves"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-296.10","depends_on_id":"bd-296","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-03-02T04:37:11Z","created_by":"import","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-296.10","depends_on_id":"bd-296.13","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-02T04:37:11Z","created_by":"import","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-296.10","depends_on_id":"bd-296.7","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-02T04:37:11Z","created_by":"import","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
@@ -226,6 +227,7 @@
 {"id":"bd-ekb3.4","title":"Audit record: constants and comments verified as correct (no change needed)","description":"## Purpose

This bead documents items that were examined during the audit and explicitly determined
to NOT need changes. This prevents future auditors from re-investigating the same items.

## Hardcoded Values Verified as Intentionally Correct

### 1. hooks.c:62 — default_wall_clock returns 0
- **Value**: `return 0;`
- **Why correct**: This is an intentional platform hook stub. The walking skeleton runtime
  provides default hooks that are replaced at runtime init. The wall_clock hook returns 0
  because deterministic mode doesn't need real time, and non-deterministic deployments
  MUST register a real clock hook via asx_runtime_hooks_init(). Returning 0 is fail-safe:
  it makes time-dependent logic behave deterministically rather than using garbage values.

### 2. lab.c:57,93 — tick_ns defaults to 1000000 (1ms)
- **Value**: `cfg->tick_ns = 1000000ULL;`
- **Why correct**: This IS a configurable default — it's set in the asx_lab_config struct
  which users fill before calling asx_lab_init(). The literal appears in config_init (setting
  the default) and in lab_init (fallback if user left it 0). Users override it by setting
  cfg.tick_ns before init. No #define needed because the config struct IS the override mechanism.

### 3. console.c:84 — "would check isatty()"
- **Value**: Comment explaining platform-specific behavior
- **Why correct**: The comment says "A real platform adapter would check isatty() and TERM
  environment variable." This is a design note explaining what the platform hook should do,
  not a promise of future work. The CORE profile doesn't have terminal awareness by design.

### 4. plan.c:413 — "this would set up a deadline"
- **Value**: Comment explaining async vs deterministic behavior
- **Why correct**: The comment says "In a real async runtime, this would set up a deadline."
  This explains that the deterministic walking skeleton doesn't use real deadlines, which
  is the intended behavior for CORE profile. Not stale — it's architecture documentation.

### 5. Profile overload thresholds in overload_catalog.c (90%, 85%, 80%, 75%)
- **Values**: Percentages like 90, 85, 80, 75
- **Why correct**: These are intentional per-profile policy constants, not magic numbers.
  Each profile defines specific overload thresholds as part of its operational envelope.
  The README explicitly says "Profiles control operational envelopes (limits, defaults,
  instrumentation), never semantic behavior." These ARE the per-profile defaults.

### 6. Seqlock EBR spike retry limits (100, 1000)
- **Values**: `max_retries = 100` and `max_spins = 1000`
- **Why correct**: The file is `seqlock_ebr_spike.c` — a research spike for experimental
  lock-free data structures. Spike code uses hardcoded values by design; if the spike
  graduates to production, the values should be extracted at that time. Premature
  abstraction of spike code adds overhead without benefit.

### 7. Config default timeouts in config_types.c (10s, 30s, 5s)
- **Values**: Various timeout defaults
- **Why correct**: These appear in config builder functions that produce default
  configurations. Users override them via the config struct before passing to init.
  The config struct IS the customization mechanism — no #define needed.

## Methodology

Each item was found via:
- `grep -rn 'TODO\|FIXME\|HACK\|XXX' src/` (zero results)
- `grep -rni ' will \| would ' src/*.c include/*.h`
- Manual inspection of all numeric literals in function bodies across runtime/, core/, net/

Items were classified as "needs fix" or "verified correct" based on:
1. Is the value user-facing or deployment-sensitive? → needs fix
2. Is there already a config mechanism? → verified correct
3. Is it a spike/research file? → verified correct (defer until graduation)
4. Is the "will/would" describing actual planned work or explaining semantics? → varies

 {"id":"bd-fjer","title":"Fix combinator deadline leak and quorum drain semantics","description":"Three semantic bugs in combinators: (1) timeout/retry_timeout never disarm deadlines from timer wheel after completing, leaking timer resources; (2) quorum combinator marks loser branches done=1 without calling branch_cancel_after_final_poll(), violating the cancellation contract that all other combinators (race, select) honor; (3) race_timeout can double-drain losers when timeout and winner declaration coincide.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-03-21T08:05:18.792349514Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-21T08:08:25.444376306Z","closed_at":"2026-03-21T08:08:25.443695968Z","close_reason":"Fixed all three combinator semantic bugs: deadline disarm on timeout/retry_timeout, quorum drain with cooperative final poll, race_timeout winner-vs-timeout priority. 134 tests passing.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["combinator","correctness"]}

 {"id":"bd-hwb","title":"EPIC: Core Semantics and Guarantee Substitution in ANSI C/C99","description":"Background:
Rust compile-time guarantees are absent in C. We must reconstitute equivalent runtime/proof guarantees via explicit mechanisms.

Goal:
Implement asx_core semantic primitives and safety scaffolding with deterministic failure behavior.

Core obligations:
- explicit state authorities
- generation-safe handles and ownership boundaries
- ghost monitors and linearity checks
- strict resource exhaustion semantics
- canonical error taxonomy and stability policy

Why this serves users:
- correctness under pressure for embedded systems
- predictable failure semantics for high-stakes systems
- confidence that performance tuning does not erase guarantees.

Cross-cutting quality contract:
- Child tasks must define and link unit tests, e2e script coverage (when behavioral), and structured log/manifest artifacts or explicit rationale for non-applicability.
- Completion evidence must remain compatible with bd-66l.9 enforcement and traceability index expectations.","status":"closed","priority":0,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2026-02-27T00:47:42.038789705Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-27T19:59:25.155834562Z","closed_at":"2026-02-27T19:59:25.155810537Z","close_reason":"done","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["core","phase-3","phase-4","safety","semantics"]}

 {"id":"bd-yx9r.9.2","title":"Implement QUIC and server connection/shutdown substrate parity","description":"Port the remaining advanced transport substrate not covered by TLS/WebSocket: QUIC streams/datagrams/connection lifecycle plus reusable server connection, accept-loop, graceful-shutdown, and connection-drain foundations. The earlier plan incorrectly implied this sibling existed; this bead makes it explicit.","design":"Keep this separate from TLS/WebSocket so review remains tractable. Be explicit about protocol/profile gating, graceful shutdown semantics, drain deadlines, retained diagnostics, and detailed structured logs for connection churn and failure cases.","acceptance_criteria":"A. Public QUIC and server-connection/shutdown surfaces exist or fail closed with documented unsupported cases.
B. Unit/integration tests cover connection lifecycle, graceful shutdown, drain deadlines, stream/datagram behavior, and failure paths.
C.
